title: Sabastian Sawe Shatters Two-Hour Barrier: A New Era in Marathon Running
summary: At the 2025 London Marathon, Kenyan athlete Sabastian Sawe, aged 30, etched his name into the annals of athletics history by becoming the first runner to complete a marathon in under two hours in a competitive race. His time of 1:59:30 not only eclipsed the previous world record of 2:00:35 set by the late Kelvin Kiptum in 2023 but also redefined the limits of human endurance. Sawe's performance was characterized by a strategic negative split: he reached the halfway point in 1:00:29, on world record pace, and then accelerated over the second half. Remarkably, debutant Yomif Kejelcha also dipped under two hours, finishing second in 1:59:41, while half marathon world record holder Jacob Kiplimo completed the podium in 2:00:28. The women's race featured Tigst Assefa of Ethiopia improving her own world record for a women-only field with a time of 2:15:41. In the wheelchair divisions, Switzerland's Marcel Hug secured his eighth London Marathon title, equaling the record of Great Britain's David Weir, while Catherine Debrunner retained her women's wheelchair crown. Sawe's achievement was the culmination of meticulous preparation and unwavering confidence; he had previously stated that breaking the record was 'only a matter of time.' This historic feat has profound implications for the sport, challenging preconceived notions about physiological limits and potentially catalyzing further advancements in training, nutrition, and technology.

keywords.explanation: A racing strategy where the second half of the race is run faster than the first half.
Surpassed or exceeded, often used in the context of records.
Causing or accelerating a change or action.
The maximum capabilities of the human body's systems during physical exertion.
Extremely careful and precise planning and training.

background_read: The marathon has a rich history dating back to the ancient Greek messenger Pheidippides. The modern marathon distance of 42.195 km (26.2 miles) was standardized in 1921. The quest to break the two-hour barrier has been a focal point of elite distance running. In 2019, Eliud Kipchoge ran 1:59:40 in the INEOS 1:59 Challenge, but this was not considered a world record due to controlled conditions (e.g., pacers, hydration on bike). Kelvin Kiptum set the official world record of 2:00:35 at the 2023 Chicago Marathon. Sabastian Sawe had previously won the 2024 London Marathon in 2:02:27. The London Marathon is part of the World Marathon Majors series. Advances in shoe technology, nutrition, and training methods have contributed to the steady improvement of marathon times.
Article_Structure: The article reports on Sabastian Sawe's historic sub-two-hour marathon at the London Marathon. Main points: Sawe's time of 1:59:30 breaks Kelvin Kiptum's record; Yomif Kejelcha also finishes under two hours; women's and wheelchair winners. Purpose: to inform of a landmark achievement in athletics. Evidence: official times, quotes from Sawe. Author credibility: BBC is a reputable news source. Methodology: reporting from the event with athlete interviews. Critical assessment: The article provides accurate information but lacks deeper analysis of factors like pacing strategy or technological influences. Strengths: clear, concise reporting with key details. Limitations: no discussion of controversies or implications for the sport.

perspectives.description: This achievement challenges previous beliefs about human physiological limits, suggesting that with optimal training and conditions, the two-hour barrier can be broken in competition.

Advances in running shoe technology, such as carbon-fiber plates and supercritical foams, may have contributed to the record-breaking performance, raising questions about fairness and the role of equipment.
